title: Rabat Review of Books
text:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2
    Rabat · Paris · London
  
  
    Subscribe
    About
    
  








  
    New Essay — Kafka in Arabic
    From the Archive — On the Art of Rereading
    Spring 2026 Issue Now Online
    Poetry — Three Poems from the Bou Regreg
    Review — The Salt Notebooks
    Subscribe to the Fortnightly Letter
  






  A Magazine of Books, Ideas & Literature
  Rabat Review of Books
  مجلّة الرباط للكُتب
  Essays, reviews, fiction, and poetry — from Rabat, for the world.
  
    Vol. I · No. 2
    Spring 2026
    Founded 2026
    Rabat · Paris · London
  




  
    
  
  
    ◆  Lead Essay  ·  Literature  ◆
    The Returning Tide: On the Private Ritual of Rereading
    Forty years on, the novels that once felt inexhaustible have quietly rearranged themselves around us. A meditation on the private ritual of rereading.
    By Mehdi Khribch · 14 March 2026 · 12-minute read
  




  ◆ Essays
  
  All essays



  
    
      
    
    The Reading Life
    Against the Algorithm: Why We Still Need Slow Reading
    A defense of the long essay, the marginal note, and the afternoon spent with a single book.
    By Mehdi Khribch
  

  
    
      
    
    Cities
    The Ghosts of Fez: Walking a City That Remembers
    A walking essay through the old medina — its doors, its libraries, its long afternoons of patient dust.
    By Mehdi Khribch
  

  
    
      
    
    Translation
    Kafka in Arabic: Translation as Second Creation
    What happens to a modernist prose when it is rewritten, word by patient word, in another alphabet.
    By Mehdi Khribch
  




  ◆  Quote of the Week  ◆
  
    A city is not the sum of its monuments. It is the residue of every sentence its inhabitants have refused to finish.
  
  — from The Ghosts of Fez, in this issue




  ◆ Reviews
  
  All reviews



  
    
      
    
    Fiction
    The Salt Notebooks
    A début novel made from family letters — lucid, difficult, and quietly essential.
    Reviewed by Mehdi Khribch
  

  
    
      
    
    History
    Atlantic Maghreb, 1400–1900
    Morocco has always been a seafaring country. A new history finally gives the shore its due.
    Reviewed by Mehdi Khribch
  

  
    
      
    
    Poetry
    Apricot Year
    A third collection, more patient — and more devastating — than anything that came before.
    Reviewed by Mehdi Khribch
  




  ◆ Fiction & Poetry
  
  All fiction



  
    
      
    
    A Short Story
    The Apricot Trees of Meknès
    A woman returns to a house she thought she had finished with. A garden, a locked drawer, a slow reckoning.
    New fiction
  

  
    
      
    
    New Poems
    Three Poems from The Bou Regreg
    A river poem sequence about the water that runs between Rabat and Salé — and between generations.
    Poetry
  




  ◆ In Three Languages
  



  
    English
    The Review believes that reading is a practice of attention — and that attention, in turn, is a small form of hospitality we offer to the world.
  
  
    Français
    La Revue croit que la lecture est une pratique de l'attention — et que l'attention, à son tour, est une petite forme d'hospitalité que nous offrons au monde.
  
  
    العربية
    تؤمن المجلّة أنّ القراءة ممارسةٌ من ممارسات الانتباه، وأنّ الانتباه بدوره شكلٌ صغير من أشكال الضيافة التي نقدّمها للعالم.
  




  ◆ From the Archive
  
  Browse archive



  
    
      
      Essay
      The Last Europeans
      On the melancholy of a Europe that, perhaps, never quite was.
    
    
      
      Memoir
      My Father's Radio
      A memoirist tunes in to the static of childhood.
    
    
      
      Novel Excerpt
      Tangier, 1956
      The last days of the International Zone, through the eyes of a British diplomat.
    
    
      
      Poetry
      Ghazals for an Absent Friend
      Two ghazal-inspired poems on distance and longing.
    
    
      
      Biography
      An Obscure Modernist
      A new biography rescues a 20th-century North African intellectual from the footnotes.
    
    
      
      Short Story
      The Bookseller of Rue du Consul
      A rare book waits thirty years for a reader.
    
  
  
    ‹
    ›
  




  The Fortnightly Letter
  A biweekly note from the editors — new essays, a forthcoming review, a poem to take with you on the commute.

title: Essays
text:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2
    Rabat · Paris · London
  
  
    Subscribe
    About
    
  








  ◆ Department
  Essays
  Long-form criticism, personal essays, and dispatches on literature & ideas.












The Last Europeans


Reading a central European novelist now.



Essays


Literature


Europe



The Central European writer who defined a continent is aging. What does his work tell us now about Europe, and about the idea of Europe itself?





Mar 28, 2026


Mehdi Khribch


2 min












Kafka in Arabic


Translation as second creation.



Essays


Translation


Literature



When Kafka’s parables appear in Arabic, they are not the same Kafka. A meditation on translation, literary ownership, and the life of texts across languages.





Mar 1, 2026


Mehdi Khribch


2 min












The Ghosts of Fez


Walking a city that remembers.



Essays


Travel


Morocco



To walk the medina of Fez is to move through layers of time. A literary walking essay on memory, urban space, and what it means to inhabit a city.





Feb 22, 2026


Mehdi Khribch


2 min












Against the Algorithm


Why we still need slow reading.



Essays


Criticism


Digital Culture



In an age of infinite scroll and algorithmic recommendation, what becomes of literary attention? A meditation on reading, time, and the future of criticism.





Feb 3, 2026


Mehdi Khribch


2 min












The Returning Tide


On rereading a writer once devoured in youth at forty.



Essays


Literature


Morocco



What does it mean to encounter a writer you loved in youth when the world has changed, and you with it? A meditation on literary memory and the Moroccan literary imagination.





Jan 15, 2026


Mehdi Khribch


2 min






No matching items

title: Poetry
text:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2
    Rabat · Paris · London
  
  
    Subscribe
    About
    
  








  ◆ Department
  Poetry
  New poems, sequences, and work in translation.












Four Short Poems


Contemporary lyrics by Laila Amara.



Poetry



A suite of four brief meditations on memory, migration, and the grammar of home.





Apr 3, 2026


Mehdi Khribch












Three Poems from The Bou Regreg


New poems by Yasmin Ighil.



Poetry



Three meditations on the river that flows through Rabat, where history and current meet.





Mar 21, 2026


Mehdi Khribch












Ghazals for an Absent Friend


Two poems by Karim Bendaoud.



Poetry



Meditations on distance and longing in the classical ghazal form.





Feb 14, 2026


Mehdi Khribch












Translations from the Arabic: Two Andalusian Fragments


Original poems in the Andalusian tradition, by Amira Lahssen.



Poetry



Two original works inspired by medieval Andalusian poetic tradition, composed in English.

Mehdi Khribch

Static and Signal


A Cairo childhood emerges through the voices of radio.



Reviews

Laila Mahmoud’s My Father’s Radio uses a single object to illuminate urban memory and family rupture.

Reviews

a contemporary poet’s poems refuse easy consolation.

title: Subscribe
text:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2
    Rabat · Paris · London
  
  
    Subscribe
    About
    
  









  ◆ Join the Review
  Subscribe
  A fortnightly letter, and an invitation to read with us.




The Review is free to read. It is sustained by readers who care about a certain kind of attention to literature — and by the quiet, old-fashioned conviction that a good essay is still worth an evening.


  
    The Letter
    Free
  
  Our fortnightly editorial letter, delivered to your inbox. New essays, notes from the editors, and one poem each issue.
  
    
    Subscribe to the Letter
  



  
    Friend of the Review
    $60 / year
  
  Everything in The Letter, plus: early access to new essays, a printed broadsheet twice a year, and an invitation to our annual reading in Rabat.
  Become a Friend



  
    Patron
    $250 / year
  
  All of the above, plus: an acknowledgement in the masthead, a complete annual set of printed editions, and the editors' quiet gratitude.
  Become a Patron

title: About the Review
text:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2
    Rabat · Paris · London
  
  
    Subscribe
    About
    
  









  ◆ About
  About the Review
  A magazine of ideas, literature, and criticism — founded in Rabat in 2026.




The Rabat Review of Books is a cosmopolitan literary magazine published from Rabat, Morocco. We commission essays, book reviews, fiction, and poetry from writers working across the Maghreb, the wider Arab world, Europe, and the Americas. Our editorial line is generous, skeptical, and unembarrassed by difficulty.

We believe that a review of books is, in the end, a practice of attention. We believe that the best reading does not resolve the world but makes its textures more available. We believe that a small magazine, published patiently, can hold the line against the flattening pressures of the feed.

What we publish

We publish long-form essays on literature, ideas, and the life of cities; reviews of recent books in English, French, and Arabic; original short fiction and novel excerpts; and poetry, including work in translation. We do not publish news, press releases, or hot takes.

A typical essay runs between 2,000 and 6,000 words. A typical review runs between 1,500 and 3,500 words. The Review is entirely written and edited by a single person, and does not pay contributors or accept unsolicited submissions.

How it is made

The Review is a one-person undertaking: essays, reviews, fiction, and poetry are written and edited by the same hand, and the magazine is published at the pace that care allows. Correspondence is welcome.

Why Rabat

Because Rabat is a city of quiet libraries, of poets on café terraces, of bougainvillea spilling over old walls. Because it faces the Atlantic and looks, without apology, at the rest of the world. Because a review of books is a practice of hospitality, and hospitality is a thing we think Rabat is especially good at.

Colophon

The Review is set in Cormorant Garamond for display text and Spectral for body text, with Amiri for Arabic. The site is built with Quarto and hosted on GitHub Pages. All editorial decisions are made in the Kasbah des Oudayas, preferably with a glass of mint tea.
